coredata.dc:title: Rapid colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for on-site detection and visualization of phosgene by amino-functionalized carbon dot-based portable droplet system
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jie
Li, Yingying
Hu, Jingyu
Sun, Yuanqiang
Yang, Ran
Li, Zhaohui
Qu, Lingbo
coredata.dc:description: Phosgene is a very dangerous but important industrial intermediate and is a significant threat to public health. There is, therefore, an urgent need to develop a rapid, visual, and on-site detection method for phosgene. Sensitive and selective phosgene responsive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by employing 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine as precursors. The amine group of the CDs can interact selectively with phosgene to effect acylation reactions, which cause color changes from yellow to amaranth and remarkable changes in fluorescence from bright yellow to orange-red due to modification of the CDs’ intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process. The CDs exhibited fast response (<2 s) for detection of phosgene with an ultralow detection limit (0.63 nM). Furthermore, a portable CD/polystyrene droplet system was developed for rapid and on-site visualization of phosgene. Specifically, the droplet system not only can realize rapid on-spot visual inspection of gaseous phosgene in the liquid phase with the naked eye, but also could make long-term visualization in the solid phase via a polystyrene membrane after solvent volatilization. This study has expanded the scope for application of CDs in sensing field, and provides a new and effective strategy for fabrication excellent platform for real-time detection of phosgene leakage in industrial and public safety applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Visualization
Phosgene
Fluorescence
Polymeric droplet
Portable sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Time-resolved fluorescence response of CDs towards phosgene
Characterization and optical properties of CDs
Optical response of CDs to phosgene in solution
Selectivity of CDs to phosgene in solution
Kinetic study on the detection of phosgene
Possible mechanism for detection of phosgene via CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and diameter distribution (inset) of the CDs (statistical quantity: 100). (b) FT-IR spectra of the CDs. (c) XPS spectra of the CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s peak (d), O 1s peak (e) and N 1s peak (f).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (b, c) Fluorescence stability of CDs under irradiation at 438 nm light and storage in the air.

(a) UV − vis absorption spectra and (b, c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (5 μg/mL) in CH2Cl2 for addition of various amounts of triphosgene (0–20 μM) recorded after 5 min; b: λex = 438 nm, slits: 5/5 nm; c: λex = 500 nm, slits: 5/5 nm, voltage: 650 V). (d) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F590/F527) for CDs (5 μg/mL) as a function of phosgene concentration (0–10 μM) in CH2Cl2. (e) Photos for color changes and (f) Fluorescence of CDs (5 μg/mL) in CH2Cl2 upon addition of various amounts of triphosgene (0–20 μM).

(a, b) Fluorescence spectra and (c) fluorescence intensity ratios (F590/F527) of the CDs (5 μg/mL) to triphosgene (10 μM) and other analytes (10 μM) for 5 min. (1) CDs, (2) DCP, (3) DECP, (4) SOCl2, (5) HCHO, (6) CH3COCl, (7) POCl3, (8) TsCl, (9) (COCl)2 and (10) Triphosgene. Inset: the color (top) and fluorescence images (bottom) of the CDs in the presence of the above analytes in CH2Cl2 solution. (d) Time-dependent fluorescence response of CDs (5 μg/mL) to triphosgene (10 μM) in CH2Cl2. λex: 438 nm, λem: 527 nm, λex: 500 nm, λem: 590 nm, slits: 5 nm/5 nm.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs and CDs-TP. (b) XPS spectra of the CDs-TP. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s peak (c), O 1s peak (d), N 1s peak (e), and Cl 2p peak (f).

(a) Color photo and (b) Fluorescence responses of the CDs/polystyrene droplets upon exposure to phosgene at different concentrations and for different time periods. (c) Color photo and fluorescence responses of CDs/polystyrene droplets upon exposure to 20 ppm of gaseous phosgene and various analytes.
Preparation of CDs and principle for visual detection of gaseous phosgene via the CDs/polystyrene droplet.

head.ce:title: Rapid colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for on-site detection and visualization of phosgene by amino-functionalized carbon dot-based portable droplet system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Safety Quick Testing and Smart Supervision Technology for State Market Regulation, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Safety Quick Testing and Smart Supervision Technology for State Market Regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Safety Quick Testing and Smart Supervision Technology for State Market Regulation, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosgene is a very dangerous but important industrial intermediate and is a significant threat to public health. There is, therefore, an urgent need to develop a rapid, visual, and on-site detection method for phosgene. Sensitive and selective phosgene responsive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by employing 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine as precursors. The amine group of the CDs can interact selectively with phosgene to effect acylation reactions, which cause color changes from yellow to amaranth and remarkable changes in fluorescence from bright yellow to orange-red due to modification of the CDs’ intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process. The CDs exhibited fast response (<2 s) for detection of phosgene with an ultralow detection limit (0.63 nM). Furthermore, a portable CD/polystyrene droplet system was developed for rapid and on-site visualization of phosgene. Specifically, the droplet system not only can realize rapid on-spot visual inspection of gaseous phosgene in the liquid phase with the naked eye, but also could make long-term visualization in the solid phase via a polystyrene membrane after solvent volatilization. This study has expanded the scope for application of CDs in sensing field, and provides a new and effective strategy for fabrication excellent platform for real-time detection of phosgene leakage in industrial and public safety applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosgene is an extremely important industrial intermediate and is widely used in the synthesis of pesticides, medicines, rubber and dyes [1]. However, it has previously been used as a chemical warfare agent (CWA) in World War I and caused tens of thousands of deaths [2]. Phosgene, a colorless, highly toxic gas, belongs to a class of acyl chloride substances, with an active carbonyl of acyl chloride, which confers electrophilic properties thus reacting with nucleophiles. Moreover, it not only has reactive centers but also has stronger reactivity compared with acyl chloride due to its unique structural characteristics [3]. Thus, phosgene reacts readily with functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and sulfhydryl groups of proteins in lung tissue, and this causes damage to the blood-air barrier, leading to increased pulmonary capillary permeability, and pulmonary edema [4,5]. Studies have indicated that an intake of 20 ppm of phosgene for more than 2 min can induced permanent lung injury and deaths can occur following exposures to 90 ppm of phosgene for 30 min [6]. Therefore, use of phosgene by terrorists as a CWA or the accidental release of the agent in industrial production, poses a huge threat to public safety. For this reason, there is an urgent need to develop a rapid, specific, real-time and visual method for detection of phosgene.

Various methods for detecting phosgene are available, including gas chromatography spectrophotometry, electrochemistry [7–9], and colorimetric and fluorescent methods. However, most analytical methods are not well suited to the detection of extremely poisonous substances due to some obvious drawbacks, such as the cost of the instrumentation, the need for skilled analysts, and the fact that the methods may be time-consuming [8,10]. Colorimetric and fluorescent detection methods have been widely adopted for detection of phosgene due to their advantages of simple operation, excellent analytical and detection performance, and practicality for on-site detection. Based on these, many robust sensors have been designed for the determination of phosgene based on the nucleophilic substitution reactions between phosgene and the probes [11,12] (see Tables S1). These reported probes provide effect strategy for the detection of phosgene. However, phosgene at normal temperatures and pressures exist in a gaseous form. It is still requirements for improvement in the sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity of detection to meet the actual needs of gaseous phosgene detection. The key is how to build an effective phosgene detection device to realize the in-situ detecting of gaseous phosgene in real scenes. Currently, probe immobilization techniques including test strips [13,14], polymeric membranes [15,16] and nanofibers [17,18] have been attempted for the gaseous phosgene detection. However, the above techniques are all by a solid–gas detection mode, and such is not conducive to fast and sensitive response due to the lower reaction properties and slower mass transfer process of the probe at the solid–gas interface. In addition, the devices manufacturing required complex process, high operation requirements and the detection performance is not be sufficiently reliable. For examples, it is very difficult for test strips making to guarantee uniform and the same amount of probe adsorption, which will influence the accuracy and repeatability of results. In addition, a large amount of probe material is wasted in the actual production process. Nanofibers preparation requires a high voltage, it is potentially dangerous and need professional personnel. In this case, it is imperative to develop novel and simple detection system and device of gaseous phosgene.

Carbon dots (CDs), a typical 0D﻿ luminescent class of nanoparticle, are endowed with excellent reactivity because the nanoparticle contains a number of surface-terminating groups [19]. Also, the CDs possess unique photoluminescence properties. When an analyte reacts with a surface group, noticeable color and fluorescence changes can be observed [20–24]. These changes in absorption and fluorescence can be attributed to the introduction of new functional groups on the surface of the CDs after the reaction, which alter the structure of the terminal groups, thus affecting the push and pull electron effect [25]. As a result, the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process of the CDs changes, and eventually leads to a red shift or a blue shift in the absorption and emission of CDs [26]. Moreover, relative to the traditional small-molecule fluorescent probes, the CDs are easy to be synthesized and their properties could be regulated by the ration design carbon source [27]. These features of the CDs meet the desired analytical requirements for development of phosgene fluorescent sensors such as speed of analysis, sensitivity, and portability. Thus, CDs as fluorescence sensors hold great potential for phosgene colorimetric and fluorescent detection.

In this work, using 2-nitro-4-aminobenzidine as the precursor, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a solvothermal method. The obtained CDs were found to exhibit an extraordinary colorimetric and fluorescence response to phosgene, with some remarkable sensing properties such as good selectivity, instant response (<2 s) and ultralow detection limit (0.63 nM). The ability of the CDs to exhibit rapid and sensitive colorimetric and fluorescence responses on contact with phosgene is due to the introduction of the electron acceptor –NO2 on the precursor, causing the push–pull electron effect between the amino and nitro groups on the CDs, which triggers ICT. Furthermore, a simple CD/polystyrene droplet system was able to be fabricated for visual detection of gaseous phosgene (Scheme 1 ). Relative to the reported solid–gas detection mode, the liquid CD/polystyrene droplet detection system was very simple and it could be established in situ and in real time just by simply coating CD/polystyrene on the desired surface. Importantly, the liquid CD/polystyrene droplet methodology matches the sensitivity of liquid phase detection, but with the additional advantages of faster response and lower detection limit. Moreover, the CDs/polystyrene droplet methodology affords not only real-time visualization of liquid phase processes but it can also realize visualization in the solid-phase via use of a polystyrene membrane support after solvent volatilization, thus facilitating color comparisons and additional analysis and fitting post detection. Thus, the CDs/polystyrene droplet methodology provides a novel and versatile route for real-time, visual and sensitive detection of phosgene. This study provides a new and effective strategy for fabrication excellent platform for real-time detection of phosgene leakage in industrial and public safety applications.

2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine, 4-Toluenesulfonyl chloride (p-TsCl), and phosphorus oxychloride (POCl3) were gifted from Energy Chemical ltd. (Shanghai, China). Triphosgene (TP) was produced by J&K Scientific ltd. (Beijing, China). Dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), oxalyl chloride ((COCl)2), thionylchloride (SOCl2), acetyl chloride (CH3COCl), diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP), and diethyl cyanophosphonate (DECP) were all from Aladdin Chemistry Co. ltd. (Shanghai, China); triethylamine (TEA), formaldehyde (HCHO), and ethanol (EtOH) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. All other reagents and solvents were not further purified.

UV − vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained by TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) and F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired using a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) and an ESCALab250 (Thermo Scientific, USA), respectively.

0.05 g of 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine was dissolved in 5 mL of EtOH and sonicated until dissolution was complete. After that, the homogeneous solution was packed in a Teflon lined autoclave and reacted for 12 h at 180 °C. Then, the autoclave was allowed to cool to ambient room temperature. The crude product was purified by passing via a silica gel column. A mixed solution of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate was used as eluent at a volume ratio of 4:5. The solid sample was obtained by removing the solvent from the purified solution by rotary evaporation. Finally, the solid CDs were dispersed in dimethyl sulfoxide to obtain 10 mg/mL stock solution.
Triphosgene and triethylamine were dissolved in CH2Cl2 to obtain a stock solution (2 mM). Stoke solutions (1 mM) of CH3COCl, (COCl)2, p-TsCl, SOCl2, POCl3, HCHO, DCP and DECP were prepared in CH2Cl2.

The experiments were performed in CH2Cl2, and the phosgene was produced in situ via the less toxic triphosgene instead of phosgene because of the high toxicity of the latter. The response of the CDs (5 μg/mL) to different concentrations of triphosgene (0–20 μM) was measured by titration. The triphosgene (2 mM) stock solution was diluted to give a series of solutions of differing concentrations. Then, 100 μL of triphosgene solution at different concentrations was added to the CH2Cl2 solutions containing CDs (5 μg/mL) with the total volume being adjusted to 2 mL for UV–vis absorption and fluorescence detection. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature. In addition, the responses of the CDs to potential interferents, including CH3COCl, (COCl)2, p-TsCl, SOCl2, POCl3, HCHO, DCP, and DECP, were also studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-resolved fluorescence response of CDs towards phosgene
ce:section.ce:para: Triphosgene was prepared in 1, 4-dioxane. The CDs (5 μg/mL) were then placed in a quartz cuvette, and triphosgene was added rapidly. The whole reaction process was monitored by real-time fluorescence.

Preparation of CDs/polystyrene solution:polystyrene (0.1 g) was completely dissolved in 5 mL of CHCl3 to obtain a transparent solution, and then 50 μL CDs (10 mg/mL) was added to form a homogeneous yellow solution. This solution was then transferred to a dropper bottle.

The CDs/polystyrene droplets were used to determine gaseous phosgene (0–35 ppm) and other potentially interfering vapors (20 ppm), including CH3COCl, (COCl)2, p-TsCl, SOCl2, POCl3, HCHO, DCP, and DECP. First, the 2 mM of triphosgene stock solution (0–119 μL, using CH3CN as solvent) and different volumes of CH3CN (765–1000 μL) were added to a 500 mL sealed container. The CDs/polystyrene solution was dripped onto a solid substrate (glass, metal, porcelain, paper or plastic) to obtain the CDs/polystyrene droplets. In this experiment, the liner of the sample bottle cap was used as the carrier. Subsequently, the above-mentioned CDs/polystyrene droplets were transferred into the above container. Next, 2 mM of triethylamine stock solution (0–119 μL, using CH3CN as solvent) were added. Among them, the molar ratio of triphosgene and triethylamine was 1:1, and the total volume of the solution was fixed at 1 mL. After a set time (1, 2 and 3 min, respectively), the sample bottle cap was removed from the container and observed in daylight or under 365 nm light, respectively. Examination of the sensing capabilities of the CDs/polystyrene droplets with respect to potential interferents (20 ppm) was performed in a similar way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs that can detect phosgene were prepared via a solvothermal method using 2-nitro-4-aminodiphenylamine as the precursor. By contrast the degree of polymerization and the initial visualization of phosgene response of CDs prepared at different time and temperature, the synthesis conditions were determined as 180 °C, 12 h, and the screening of specific CDs synthesis conditions is provided in the supporting data (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs are spherical with size ranged from 2 to 6.5 nm, and an average size of about 4.3 nm.The functional groups of the CDs via FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1b). The peaks at 1644 cm−1 belonged to CO on the secondary amide. The stretching vibration of CH is responsible for peaks at 2922 and 2852 cm−1. Peaks at 1599 and 1510 cm−1 testify to the presence of a benzene ring. The peak at 1483 cm−1 is attributed to the nitro group. The peaks at 1132 and 1050 cm−1 correspond to CC and CO bonds. All the above results show that decomposition and condensation reactions take place during the preparation of the CDs. The elemental composition of CDs was analyzed by XPS spectroscopy. The XPS responses for the CDs originate mainly from C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s (Fig. 1c). The prepared CDs contain 74.71 % C, 15.89 % O and 9.4 % N. In the high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 1d − f), the C 1s is present in three peaks: C C/CC (284.8 eV), C O/C N (285.9 eV), and CO (288.0 eV). The N 1s spectrum contains four peaks at 398.1 eV, 399.1 eV, 400.2 eV and 405.0 eV, indicating the presence of amino N, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and –NO2. These showed that the CDs contain amino and nitro groups. On the one hand, the amino group, as the recognition group, can react specifically with phosgene. In addition, the amino group with a lone electron pair, as the electron donor, can form a push–pull electron system via the strong electron-withdrawing CO and –NO2 groups which result in an effective enhanced ICT process in the CDs. Similarly, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s can be described by two peaks. The peaks at 531.3 eV for CO and 531.9 eV for CO/OH were observed. In summary, the results of FT-IR and XPS showed that the CDs contain abundant amino groups, which provides the basis for the detection of phosgene.

The optical properties of CDs (Fig. 2 a) showed that the emission wavelength of the CDs at 527 nm remained unchanged with the excitation wavelength changing gradually from 388 nm to 488 nm, indicating that the CDs have only one emission center and excitation-independence. The fluorescence stability of CDs under irradiation at 438 nm light for 30 min and the time-stored fluorescence stability of stock solution placed in the air for 3 months were investigated respectively (Fig. 2b and c). Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change significantly either under 438 nm light or in the air for a long time. The results show that the CDs have good photostability and photobleaching resistance in sunlight, room temperature, which is also the basis for the following CDs colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection of phosgene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response of CDs to phosgene in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Given that the CDs are endowed with numerous nitrogen-containing groups (e.g., amino, pyridinic N), they can catalyze the decomposition of triphosgene into phosgene [28,29]. Therefore, there was no need to add triethylamine in the subsequent experiments, and the phosgene was replaced directly with triphosgene (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption band of the CDs at 435 nm decreases and a new absorption peak appears at 500 nm with the increase of triphosgene. Meanwhile, the color of the solution changed from light yellow to pink (Fig. 3e). The fluorescence results are presented in Fig. 3b and c. The CDs have a prominent emission peak at 527 nm. After the addition of triphosgene, the emission peak of the CDs continues to weaken. A new emission peak is generated at 590 nm, accompanied by a change in the color of fluorescence from yellowish-green to orange-red (Fig. 3f). It is believed that the redshift in the absorption and emission spectra is attributed to the enhanced ICT, which is owing to the increase in the efficiency of the push–pull electron effect due to the introduction of CO after reaction with phosgene. The above experimental results demonstrate clearly that the detection of phosgene by CDs may be realized by both colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence. Further, it was noted that the logarithm of the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at the optimum emission site (log F590/F527) for the CDs and the reaction product exhibit excellent linear relationships with triphosgene in the concentration range 0–10 μM (R2 = 0.9990) (Fig. 3d). According to the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3), the detection limit is 0.63 nM which is much higher sensitive than most of the reported methods. The above results suggest that the prepared CDs can serve as fluorescence probes for the determination of phosgene at trace/ultratrace levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs to phosgene in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Phosgene is a very active electrophilic reagent, which is prone to nucleophilic substitution reaction. The selectivity of the probe mainly depends on the electrophilicity of the interferences. So, according to possible coexisting interferences with high electrophilicity, including CH3COCl, (COCl)2, p-TsCl, SOCl2, POCl3, HCHO, DCP, and DECP were selected in this work. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, when the CDs react with triphosgene and other analytes, a new emission peak at 590 nm is produced only on the addition of triphosgene, and this is accompanied by a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 527 nm. Meanwhile, the color of the solution changed distinctly from pale yellow to pink, and the fluorescence changed from yellow-green to orange-red (Fig. 4c). In contrast, when other analytes are present in the CD solution, a change in the color or the fluorescence was not observed, and the fluorescence response recorded was consistent with the emission peak corresponding to that of the CDs. All these proved the high selectivity of the CDs towards phosgene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic study on the detection of phosgene
ce:section.ce:para: The extent of poisoning from exposure to phosgene depends on the concentration and duration of exposure. Therefore, the rate of response of the CDs to phosgene is a key parameter to evaluate it actual application. The response of the CDs to triphosgene was studied by monitoring the fluorescence versus time responses at 527 and 590 nm after the addition of triphosgene. Fig. 4d shows that the CDs respond very quickly to phosgene and the probe can effectively attain a steady-state within 2 s after the addition of triphosgene, meaning that the CDs can be used as a fluorescence probe for rapid monitoring of phosgene in real-time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for detection of phosgene via CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate possible mechanisms for the reaction between the CDs and phosgene, FT-IR and XPS were used to analyze the reaction products. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the peak at 3420 cm−1 is significantly weakened after reaction with phosgene. Furthermore, the NH peak of the secondary amide at 3147 cm−1 and the CO peak at 1634 cm−1 were relatively enhanced, indicating that the primary and secondary amino groups on the surface of the CDs generated a secondary and tertiary amide, respectively, after the reaction of CDs with phosgene. Most importantly, the appearance of the CO peak at 1727 cm−1 on the FT-IR spectrum of the product indicates that NH2 and NH on the surface of the CDs had reacted with phosgene to form a five-membered ring urea derivative. The XPS spectrum of the product also confirmed that the CDs had reacted with phosgene. Fig. 5b showed that the peak for Cl appeared in the product compared with that for the CDs, and Fig. 5f demonstrated that Cl existed mainly in the Cl- form, which suggested that the amino group had reacted with phosgene to generate hydrochloric acid (HCl) [30]. The FT-IR and XPS comparisons of CDs and products suggest that the fluorescence response mechanism of the CDs toward phosgene may be as follows: the electron-rich groups of amino and hydroxyl and the electron withdrawing group of –NO2 formed push–pull electron system, thus triggering ICT and causing bright fluorescence. When CDs react with phosgene, the introduction of CO increases the number of electron acceptor on the surface of CDs and promotes the increase of electron transfer. Therefore, the ratio of ICT in the mixed excited state is enhanced accordingly, resulting in the red shift of absorption and emission. Ultimately, the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection of phosgene is realized.

It is far from meeting the actual detection requirements for just owning to the phosgene probe with excellent performance in solution, because phosgene in the scene exists in a gaseous state. Nowadays, test strips, polymeric membranes and nanofibers are the main detection modes for the phosgene detection. However, the devices are all by a solid–gas detection mode and not conducive to fast and sensitive response due to the lower reaction properties and slower mass transfer process of the probe at the solid–gas interface. In addition, the devices manufacturing required complex process, high operation requirements and the detection performance is not be sufficiently reliable. In order to overcome the above shortcomings, a droplet detection system was attempted. The detection system only needs to blend the CDs and polystyrene, and then coat them on desired solid surface to form a detection film. For this detection system, no equipment and manufacturing process is required which greatly reduces the inaccurate detection results and poor reproducibility caused by the difference between batches of detection devices.

As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, when the droplets were exposed in the phosgene with different concentrations, the color of the droplets gradually changed from yellow to amaranth and the fluorescence changed from bright yellow to orange-red. The color change of the droplets for different detection times showed that discernible variations in color were observed by the naked eye for a response time of 1 min when the concentrations of phosgene were ≥ 20 ppm (Fig. 6a and b). The color contrast can be further enhanced when the detection time is increased to 2 or 3 min (Fig. 6a and b). This liquid state-based detection method increased the response speed and the contact area between the CDs/polystyrene droplets and phosgene. This approach is advantageous for the adsorption of gaseous phosgene and improves the sensitivity of the CDs for detection of gaseous phosgene. Moreover, the droplet could make long-term visualization in the solid phase via a polystyrene membrane after solvent volatilization, which can prolong the observation time and make the fluorescence phenomenon more distinct. Given the excellent portability of the CDs/polystyrene droplet probe system and the vivid color changes, the system has immense potential for rapid on-site and visual detection of phosgene in practical industrial scenarios.

In order to further prove that CDs/polystyrene droplets have better analytical performance than the traditional detection mode based on the solid devices, the test strip and polymeric membrane of the CDs also were made. We compared the daylight and fluorescence phenomena of 20 ppm phosgene detected for 2 min by the test strip, polymeric membrane and polystyrene droplet prepared at the same concentration of CDs. As shown in the Fig. S3, the degree of response varies greatly, but just only the droplets have obvious color change. The results demonstrate that the detection ability of CDs/polystyrene droplet is significantly improved in both response speed and visualization compared with solid-state devices. Hence, CDs/polystyrene droplet has higher sensitivity, which can detect phosgene in time and give visual signals that can be readily observed by naked eyes. Altogether, CDs/polystyrene droplets can indeed more suitable for the detection of highly toxic phosgene than solid-state devices.

Given the possibility of the co-existence of potential interferents during an on-site analysis, the selectivity of the CDs/polystyrene droplet system was tested for exposure to 20 ppm phosgene and other selected analytes for 2 min. As shown in Fig. 6c, the noticeable color and fluorescence changes may be readily observed for exposure to phosgene such that the variations in color confirmed the presence of phosgene. In contrast, in the case of all other analytes, the droplet system did not exhibit significant color changes confirming that the system possessed good selectivity for phosgene. These results demonstrate that CDs/polystyrene droplets can be served as a convenient and effective tool to visually detect phosgene with high specificity and sensitivity.

A facile method for the detection of phosgene based on the interplay between the amine group of the CDs and phosgene via an acylation reaction that induced changes in the color and fluorescence of the CDs has been developed. The CD method relies on a rational modulation of the ICT process. On instigation of the reaction, the proportion of NH moieties on the CDs decreased significantly. Moreover, owing to the addition of the electron-withdrawing CO group, the capability to accept electrons was increased, thus enhancing the push–pull electron inductive effect on the CDs. The peak maximum for the absorption and emission after the reaction exhibited a red-shift. Based on the remarkable changes in the optical properties combined with the rapidity of the reaction, the CDs exhibited highly selective colorimetric and fluorescent responses for phosgene together affording fast response times (<2 s) and an ultralow detection limit (0.63 nM). Moreover, the CDs were readily fabricated into CDs/polystyrene droplets, which are cost-effective, highly efficient, and portable, enabling almost instantaneous visualization of gaseous phosgene with high sensitivity in situ and on-site. On the one hand, the method can visualize gaseous phosgene via the liquid droplet methodology and, on the other hand, when the solvent is volatilized, the system effectively functions as a polystyrene membrane, offering long-term visualization potential commensurate with other solid-phase sensor devices. In short, the developed method provides a portable, economic and highly efficient means for detection of gaseous analytes in an industrial environment and extends the field of application of carbon dots to detection of highly toxic substances such as phosgene.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1804136) and the Zhongyuan Scholars Program (202101510005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.139173.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
